data.title: M2 Update: Okay For Now, But The Fed Should Ease
data.description:
This blog has the distinction of periodically tracking and analyzing the M2 measure of money supply for almost 2 decades. Unlike most other analysts, I focus not just on the supply of money but also on the demand for money. For background, see this post from October 2020 in which I noted rapid growth in M2 but was not alarmed given the huge increase in money demand at the time.

The key to understanding the relationship between money and inflation is to not lose sight of the demand for money. Milton Friedman famously taught us that inflation happens only when the supply of money exceeds the demand for it.

When the M2 money supply accelerated in 2009 and 2020-21, it did not immediately ignite inflation because the demand for money also accelerated - with Covid shutdowns, it was quite difficult to spend money.

But when the demand for money began to decline in early 2021, the money that had been stored in bank deposits and under mattresses began to be released into the economy, and this was the fuel for rising inflation in 2021 and into early 2022.

By mid-2022, the Fed had responded to rising inflation by raising interest rates and slowing the growth of M2, thus bringing money supply and demand slowly back into balance. This gave me the confidence in the summer of 2022 to predict that the peak of inflation was now past and it would begin to decline.

Since then I have continued to believe that inflation would remain low, given that although the demand for money was declining to pre-Covid levels, M2 growth remained very slow.

The charts that follow are updated with the latest data. Fortunately, there has been no significant change in monetary conditions, and that is why I continue to see inflation remaining relatively low and stable.

Chart #1

Chart #1 shows the level of the M2 money supply. From 1995 to late 2019, it grew by about 6% per year. During that same period, inflation registered about 2% per year. M2 growth then exploded in early 2020, as $6 trillion of transfer payments were sent to the public to compensate for Covid-related shutdowns - money that the Fed essentially "printed" and helicopter-dropped into the economy.

M2 began to slow in mid-2022 as the Fed began to raise interest rates. Today M2 is only $1.3 trillion above where it might have been had we not suffered from the government's disastrous Covid policies and the Fed's failure to react to huge swings in money demand in a timely fashion.

Chart #2

Chart #2 shows the 6-mo. annualized rate of change in the M2 money supply. Growth has been below 6% since April '22, almost 4 years. Over the past year, M2 has grown only 4.9%. Milton Friedman would be pleased.

Chart #3

Chart #3 shows the ratio of M2 to nominal GDP, which is arguably a good proxy for money demand. Think of it as the percentage of our annual income that we collectively prefer to hold in the form of readily spendable money (currency, checking accounts, time deposits, CDs, and money market funds).

Money demand appears to have returned just about to where it was before the Covid era. Both M2 growth and money demand have now returned to "normal" levels, and they are roughly in balance. This is very good.

Chart #4

But, you object, inflation is still above the Fed's 2% target. Yes, the Core Personal Consumption Deflator is up 3.1% in the year ending January '26, and the overall Personal Consumption Deflator is up 2.7%.

But the CPI is up only 2.4% as of February, and the CPI less shelter is up only 2.2%. We're not talking about a meaningful overshoot. Of course, I would prefer to see 0% inflation, but what we have today is the next best thing.

Chart #4 shows the 3 major components of the PCE deflator. Note that durable goods prices today are lower than they were in late 2022, and non-durable goods prices have only increased 2% since mid-2022 (which works out to an annualized rate of only 0.6%).

The major source of inflation in recent years comes from service sector prices, which are heavily influenced by shelter costs and wages. As I've shown numerous times in recent years, the government has been systematically overestimating shelter costs. The PCE deflator doesn't have an ex-shelter version like the CPI does; if it did, it would likely show inflation being much closer to 2% than to 3%.

So what about the Iran war and soaring fuel prices? Yesterday I paid $7.09 per gallon to fill my tank. Yikes! Surely this will upset the inflation apple cart, no?

Most likely we'll see an upward bump in the official inflation numbers in the months ahead, given the recent increase in energy prices. But that's not how inflation works. With the economy on a strict diet of 5-6% money growth, there is no reason to expect that the entire price level will jump higher.

Energy prices will go up, but other prices will come down. Already we're seeing non-energy commodity prices softening. And with the recent uptick in 30-yr mortgage rates and the surge in new homes for sale, real estate prices are more likely to decline than to rise.

Most important, however, is this: wartime uncertainties are very likely to spark an increase in money demand. Without a corresponding increase in money supply - and especially since the Fed shows no willingness to lower interest rates - the economy will soon feel starved for money. That's deflationary. We won't see deflation for several months, but don't be surprised if it happens later this year.

My advice to the Fed would be to relax monetary policy soon in anticipation of a meaningful increase in money demand. But sadly, the Fed is usually reactive and rarely - if ever - proactive.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: HPS: Decent Yield For Retirees That Want To Protect Capital
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund III (HPS), I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation at the time. Since then, the market sentiment has drastically shifted. Market indices pulled back from their all-time highs, global tensions are rising, and the unemployment rate has continued to trend higher. Due to these influences, HPS's share price has fluctuated a bit over the last few quarters. Despite those challenges, I believe that HPS is still a solid choice for investors seeking a source of reliable income generation. More specifically, I wanted to revisit the fund to highlight the reasons why HPS can be a great option for retired investors seeking stability.

Even with the volatility in the market, HPS still trades at a reasonable valuation. For instance, the fund now trades at a small premium to NAV of 2.62%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund still trades within the middle of its historical price to NAV range. For instance, HPS has traded at an average premium to NAV of 4.34% over the last five year period, which is confirmation that this is still a great time to accumulate shares.

The fund has a solid track record of preserving capital over time, but there are some tradeoffs that must be considered. For instance, HPS likely won't be a great fit for investors that want to maximize total returns over a longer holding period. Furthermore, HPS is concentrated within the financial sector, which means that the fund may be vulnerable to sector specific risks. Although the fund offers a high dividend yield of 9.3%, the payouts may not offer tax-efficiency. So let's start by taking a look at the strategy HPS utilizes to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, HPS has total net assets of $442.2M that are spread across 166 different holdings. The fund is unique because it provides characteristics of both equities and bonds by focusing on preferred stocks and convertible securities. This approach is great for retirees because preferred securities sit right between common equities and debt securities. Therefore, preferred securities get paid before common shareholders, while also providing a layer of safety. This unique structure also tends to result in less volatility over time, which is great for investors that have the primary goal of preserving capital over time.

The only issue is that the fund is most heavily allocated to financial positions at 37.97% of assets. While this only makes up a little more than a third of total assets, this specific allocation may introduce some concentration risks. If we ever see a scenario where the financial sector goes through any specific headwinds, such as any large banks facing a default, which could negatively impact the fund's sentiment.

Another part of the fund's strategy that pairs well with retirees is the large focus on investment grade securities. For context, investment grade securities are positions with a credit rating of BBB and above. These are typically the strongest type of borrower that has consistent cash flows and aren't heavily reliant on debt to fuel operations. This larger allocation to investment grade securities helps provide better price stability during this time of higher interest rates. For reference, the fund's breakdown by investment grade is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 59.95%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 36.44%
  • Not Rated: 1.78%

When assessing the holdings of HPS, we can see that the fund provides exposure to some of the highest quality businesses. For instance, the largest individual position is in Bank of America (BAC) at 6.40% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable names, including Morgan Stanley (MS), Citigroup (C), CMS Energy (CMS), and Wells Fargo (WFC), just to name a few. However, we must remember that HPS has this exposure through preferred securities so the fund may not be able to directly participate in the growth of its holdings. However, retirees may find this positioning attractive since HPS maintains this exposure higher up on the corporate capital structure.

Performance Expectations For Retirees

I wanted to put emphasis on the fact that HPS is best suited for retirees seeking a reliable source of income generation. The fund may not be structurally aligned to capture upside growth of the market, which means that it may not be a great fit for investors looking to maximize total return.

For instance, market indices raced to all-time highs over the last decade but HPS's share price has declined by nearly 23.8% over the same time frame. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 71.4% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Despite this, the fund's lack of exposure to common equities means that HPS can provide stability during times of uncertainty. For instance, market indices have been trending downward over the last few months due to the continued selloff of the technology sector. The threat of AI continues to cause investors to question the moat for many of the large-cap software companies. So as the S&P 500 (SPY) experiences choppiness, HPS has provided much more stability for investors. On a YTD basis, SPY has experienced a total loss of nearly 4%. However, HPS has been able to preserve investor capital and still manages to scrape by with a positive total return.

Retirees must be comfortable with this dynamic over a longer holding period. If you feel like you wouldn't want to miss out on capital appreciation, a more standard index-tracking ETF may be a better fit for you. For instance, the State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) provides diversified exposure across the financial industry and it may be the better choice for the investor looking to maximize total return.

Lastly, HPS's high dividend yield of 9.3% doesn't automatically translate to high returns over time. Since the fund is paying out most of its earnings as a distribution, this actually works against its ability to keep up with the pace of the financial sector. There may be some periods where the fund is faced with the challenge of paying out more than it actually earns, which leads to NAV erosion. When comparing the total return of HPS against XLF, we can see that HPS's total return of ~71.4% underperforms against XLF's total return of 228.1% over the last ten years.

Risks

Despite the fund's long track record of success, there are some risks for investors to consider. I believe one of the largest risks are related to the fund's use of leverage. The use of leverage now amounts to 37.34% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $292.3M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment because it introduces sensitivity to rate fluctuations. For instance, a higher interest rate environment negatively impacts the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from its securities.

The smaller operating spread can suppress HPS's earnings growth and threaten the stability of the share price. Despite the Fed initiating several cuts to interest rates throughout 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the Fed's latest meeting, it was decided to keep interest rates unchanged. If we face a scenario where future interest rate cuts are delayed, this could put pressure on the fund's earnings potential and increase the risk of being impacted by defaults.

Steady Monthly Dividends

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.11 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.3%. Converted to an annual payout, HPS is estimated to issue total distributions of $1.32 per share over the next twelve months. The monthly income can be great for retirees that use the dividend income received to support lifestyle expenses. Looking at the payout history over the last decade, the fund has maintained a solid track record of consistent payouts. Although there was a distribution reduction in mid-2019, the fund has been able to keep payouts consistent since then.

However, I do remain a bit cautious about the fund's ability to maintain its current payout if interest rates do not get reduced over the next twelve months. Although the latest annual report for 2025 is a bit dated, I wanted to refer to the report to demonstrate how the higher interest rates have suppressed earnings. For instance, the fund generated only $1.22 per share worth of holdings for its 2025 period, which wasn't quite enough to support the distributions being paid. However, the missed earnings wasn't that far off so the NAV erosion was minimal.

Net investment income should substantially improve once interest rates decline and improve the operating spread. For instance, 2025's net investment income landed at $0.94 per share. When interest rates were much lower in 2021, HPS's net investment income amounted to $1.25 per share due to the healthier operating spread at the time. Going forward, I would love to see net investment income substantially improve over the next few years so that it can support distributions on its own without relying on net realized gains.

Although HPS hasn't provided any sort of traditional dividend growth, the fund's consistency should still be commended. Long-term investors would still be able to create their own dividend growth by staying invested and reinvesting dividends. To demonstrate this, I ran a back test of a $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. This back test assumes that no additional capital was invested after the initial $100K but it does assume that all dividends were reinvested.

2017 Dividend Income: $8,574

2025 Dividend Income: $14,815

However, retirees need to consider the fact that the distributions received from HPS may not offer tax-efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 77% of the payouts paid YTD were supported by net investment income, which is classified as ordinary dividends and has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in HPS is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that HPS remains an attractive buy at its current price to NAV valuation. The fund has an excellent track record of preserving capital over time since it specifically focuses on preferred securities. The portfolio is aligned to provide price stability through periods of market uncertainty like we are currently experiencing. So if market indices continue to sell off through 2026, HPS may be a great defensive position for investors seeking income. Conversely, the fund may also underperform through a stronger bull market once interest rates are reduced closer to their pre-pandemic levels. A lower interest rate environment can improve the fund's operating spread and reduce the negative impact of its leverage.

data.title: 20 March Dogcatcher Favorite Toy Dog Dividend Fetchers
data.description:
Foreword

Investors have ignored dividend stocks for the greater part of three years while visions of other investing sugarplums danced in their heads.

Money market funds and Treasuries offered better yields for no risk, for a start, and then came the artificial intelligence (AI) boom in growth stocks, all of which "sucked the capital out of the rest of the market," says Jay Hatfield, chief executive of Infrastructure Capital Advisors and manager of the InfraCap Equity Income exchange-traded fund.

Little wonder, then, that dividend stocks have lagged the S&P 500 in recent years.

But the tide seems to be turning, in part because interest rates [came] down, which helped make dividend stocks more appealing. Hatfield says. "We think this trend will continue."

Indeed, investors have forgotten some truisms about dividend-stock investing. Over the long haul, for one, dividend-paying stocks have outpaced non-dividend payers, and they've been less volatile, too, says John Buckingham, editor of the investing newsletter The Prudent Speculator.

"Everyone's holy grail is higher returns and lower risk, and it's sitting right in front of us with dividend-paying stocks," he says.

—BY NELLIE S. HUANG, Kiplinger Investing

This collection of Dogcatcher Attractive Toy Dogs is low priced, and 20 reveal ideal dividends supported by free cash flow. Sadly, March showed twenty-four Toy Dogs that failed the safety check. Either those twenty-four “losers” had negative net income for the year or their free cash flow was inadequate to pay dividends; that meant paying dividends with borrowed money.

With renewed downside market pressure to $15 or 62%, it could be possible for thirty of the forty-four top-yield YCharts “attractive” toy dogs to become elite fair-priced, with their annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of the top ten fair-priced March YCharts Attractive Toy Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the “safer” choice and tag-alongs found by digging deeper into the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 26.20% to 62.00% Net Gains From Top-Ten Attractive Toy Dogs by March 2027

Four of the ten top Attractive Toy Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this March, 2026 yield-based forecast for them, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2026-7 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to March 2027 were:

Regional Management (RM) was projected to net $620.05 based on the median of target prices estimated by 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 7% above the market as a whole.

Graphic Packaging Holding Company (GPK) was projected to net $599.43, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 35% under the market as a whole.

NewtekOne, Inc. (NEWT) was projected to net $491.23, based on the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 12% over the market as a whole.

Medallion Financial Corp. (MFIN) was projected to net $451.17, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 22% less than the market as a whole.

Apogee Enterprises, Inc. (APOG) was projected to net $388.68, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 9% over the market as a whole.

OP Bancorp (OPBK) was projected to net $352.76, based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 34% less than the market as a whole.

Reynolds Consumer Products Inc. (REYN) was projected to net $298.65, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 43% under the market as a whole.

Provident Financial Services, Inc. (PFS) was projected to net $269.45, based on dividends plus the median of target price estimates from 5 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 14% under the market as a whole.

Donegal Group Inc. (DGICA) was projected to net $266.23 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 95% under the market as a whole.

United Community Banks (UCB) was projected to net $262.00 based on the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed risk/volatility 16% under the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 39.99% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Attractive Toy Dog stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 15% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks are dividend dogs when they show three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Thus, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest-yielding stocks in any collection become valued as "dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be, in fact, best named "underdogs," owing to their contrarian nature.

Top 44 March, 2026 Attractive Toy Dogs by Analyst Targets

March 44 Attractive Toy Dogs by Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top March Attractive Top Dog Yields Ranged 4.43% to 6.77% Per YCharts

Top ten Attractive Toy Dogs by yield occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Six financial representatives placed first, fifth, sixth, seventh, ninth, and tenth: NewTekOne, Inc. [1], First Interstate BancSystem, Inc. (FIBK) [5], Medallion Financial Corp. [6], Farmers National Banc Corp. (FMNB) [7], Provident Financial Services Inc. [9], and Norwood Financial Corp. (NWFL) [10].

In second place was the lone industrial member, Global Ship Lease, Inc. (GSL) [2]. Then two utilities sector stocks took the third and fourth positions, Suburban Propane Partners, L.P. (SPH) [3], and Star Group, L.P. (SGU) [4].

Thereafter, one materials member placed eighth, Graphic Packaging Holding Co. [8], to complete the March Attractive Toy Dogs by yield.

Dividend vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten Attractive Toy Dogs by dividend and price as of market close 3/20/26.

This month all of the top-ten Attractive Toy Dogs by yield show ideal status because their prices are less than projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

Attractive Toy Dog Costs Per Dividend Dollar as of 3/20/26

The lower chart above reveals the remaining thirty-four Toy dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the Attractive Toys is NewTekOne, Inc., at $14.78 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is Insteel Industries, Inc. (IIIN), at $255.10 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Five of Forty-Four Attractive Toy Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. So their dividends are paid with borrowed money. The five Toy Dogs so infected are Suburban Propane, Graphic Packaging, BRC Resources, Radian Group, and Insteel Industries.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pullback opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, deals, wars, tariffs, and negotiations, too.

All Ten Top Yield Attractive Toy Dogs Show "Fair Price" Rates for Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust the share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, the top ten are already ideally priced. Besides those top ten, another ten attractive Toy Dog stocks are right there, too.

Two alternatives are available for stragglers. Companies could raise their dividends or split shares. Those choices may be too risky in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile trade- and war-torn times. Mr. Market must be far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts; just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot. For example, four are within pennies of crossing into IDEAL territory: Orrstown Financial; Sierra Bancorp; Radian Group; and SM Energy.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 6.86% To 18.55% Top Ten Attractive Toy Dog Upsides To March 2027 (32). One -15.42% Downsider Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target estimates provided another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Forecast a 47.36% Advantage for 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Attractive Toy Dogs as of March 20, 2026

Ten top Attractive Toy Dogs were culled by yield for this monthly update. Yield (dividend/price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted above, the top ten Toys selected on 3/10/26 by yield represented four of eleven business sectors. Therefore, communication services, consumer discretionary, consumer staples, health care, technology, real estate, and utilities missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Kiplinger Favorite Dividend Dogs (34) to Deliver 38.59% vs. (35) 28.19% Net Gains by All Ten Come March 20, 2027

$5,000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Attractive Toy Dogs by yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 47.36% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all ten. The second lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Graphic Packaging, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 59.94%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield Attractive Toy Dogs for March were Medallion Financial; Graphic Packaging; NewTekOne; Star Group; Farmers National Banc, with prices ranging from $8.37 to $12.59.

Five higher-priced Attractive Toy Dogs as of March 20 were Suburban Propane; Provident Financial; Norwood Financial; First Interstate BancSystem; and Global Ship Lease, with prices ranging from $20.10 to $37.80.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As holiday euphoria dies and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential wars, tariffs, deals, and threats gain momentum into 2026. With the exception of a few hot ticket stocks, look for forty of the top Attractive Toy Dogs to become fair-priced… slowly but surely, or perhaps quite suddenly.

Afterword

March Attractive Toy Dogs

In late March 2026, 26 of the 44 Attractive Toy Dogs were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow yields greater than dividend yields. YCharts 5/20/26 data showed twenty IDEAL fair-priced

How Aristocrat's "Safer" Dividend vs. Price Results Produced a "Fair Price" Result for Investors

The chart at the top above reveals dividends from $1K invested in all of our top 'safer' Attractive Toy Dogs, which conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. The dollar amount and percentage change required to drop to the break-even point are displayed.

The middle chart shows the recent prices, and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to the single share price of each stock, a so-called “break-even price.”

A market correction of only $15, or 62%, could put all ten share prices of these “safer” survivors equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Kiplinger Favorite Dividend updates out here in public, away from the paywalled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

Attractive Toy Dogs: 26 "Safer" + 20 Best Buys 5/20/26

The above chart lists the 2026 twenty Dogcatcher Attractive Toy "Buy" Dogs.

Net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

data.title: Columbia Quality Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Bond investors were rewarded with positive returns across the U.S. bond market during the fourth quarter.

Fund performance

  • Columbia Quality Income Fund Institutional Class shares returned 0.97% for the three months ending December 31, 2025, outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS) Index, which returned 1.71% for the same period.

Market overview

Bond investors were rewarded with positive returns across the U.S. bond market during the fourth quarter. With indicators suggesting that economic growth was softening, the U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease interest rates, with two additional 25-basis-point (bps) cuts at its October and December meetings, leaving the federal funds target rate in the 3.50% to 3.75% range. (A basis point is 1/100 of one percent.) The Fed also announced an end to its quantitative tightening program, under which it has reduced the size of its balance sheet by not reinvesting the proceeds from maturing bonds. The Fed cited a softening employment backdrop but also indicated caution with respect to additional rate cuts, given lingering inflation concerns. The Treasury yield curve steepened slightly over the quarter, while the 10-year Treasury yield finished essentially flat, moving from 4.16% to 4.18%.

Against this backdrop, securitized assets outperformed, with mortgage-backed securities (MBS) continuing to benefit from lower interest-rate volatility and prepayment risk. The Bloomberg US MBS Index generated a 1.71% total return during the fourth quarter, supported by strong carry, reduced prepayment risk and positive technical signals. Demand improved from real-estate investment trusts (REITs) and government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs), which got relief on the number of mortgages they could hold on to their balance sheets. This uptick in demand was coupled with continued low supply, given tough affordability metrics. Inventories continued to rise. Existing homes available for sale were up 15% year over year as home prices appreciated, albeit at a much lower rate.

Non-agency MBS also performed well during the quarter, benefiting from a continued appetite for risk assets and higher yields, plus stable performance across most regions. Investors enjoyed historically low principal losses, largely owing to home equity at the highest levels ever. According to a recent Fitch study, principal losses in non-qualified mortgages originated since 2015 have been 3 bps, while home equity exceeds $35 trillion nationally.

Consumer health, especially at the subprime end of the spectrum, has dominated the conversation, with a renewed focus on underwriting standards. New asset-backed securities (ABS) issuance surged to its highest level since 2016, and defaults such as Tricolor, an issuer we had no exposure to, were a strong reminder of our value proposition, which is centered on research intensity.

In commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), idiosyncratic risk remained elevated. Defaults stabilized going into year-end, and the overall tone for office mortgages improved at the margin. Weakness in near-term maturities continued to be a theme. This remains a sector to which we have very low exposure.

Top sector weights (%) as on December 31, 2025

Attribution

  • Performance in the month of December was the most significant detractor given an underweight to the agency MBS sector in favor of collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) which struggled as the market repriced future interest rate cuts on stronger economic data.
  • Duration and curve positioning also worked against the fund's performance for a similar reason.
  • Allocations to non-agency MBS and CMBS were positive given lower volatility and a continued appetite for risk.

Outlook

Overall, it was a strong year for the structured credit sector and the performance of the fund. Total return was 8.58% for the Bloomberg US MBS index, with the MBS sector our most significant holding. The sector beat the broad Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index by roughly 130 bps. This performance was achieved despite banks reducing their holdings by roughly $57 billion — an outcome most investors did not expect. The positive catalyst came in the form of lower volatility. Also positive was the continued trend of negative net issuance in Fannie- and Freddie-originated mortgages, so-called “conventionals”. GSE reform was all talk, no action, but the agencies’ appetites for higher allocations to MBS on their balance sheets was net positive. Over the course of the year, prepay speeds were strongly tied to rate rallies, no matter how brief. The laggards across the sector were Ginnie Mae MBS. The MBS sector was negatively impacted by higher supply and faster speeds. Builder buydowns was also a theme, as inventories rose and resulted in more discount coupons.

We have conviction in agency MBS but recognize that the compression in spreads leaves less room for the same level of tightening going forward. That said, the recent announcement from the Trump administration to purchase about $200 billion of agency MBS has shifted the value proposition and is a potential catalyst for stronger returns in the months ahead. Structural tailwinds of higher mortgage rates and low refinancing volumes remain in place, and the potential for new investors is creating some energy. While Fed balance sheet runoff is slow and will be reinvested in Treasury bills, the MBS sector is gaining traction from REITs and GSEs. Furthermore, the Federal Housing Finance Agency raised Freddie’s $20 billion MBS investment limit to $40 billion and now allows CMOs to be held as well. More potential for the GSEs to be buyers in an environment of zero net new production is ultimately a positive. Volatility continuing to decline could also bring back bank demand.

Broadly speaking, prospects for bonds in 2026 look strong but not without hurdles. With proactive rate cuts by the Fed, resilient corporate fundamentals and continued investor appetite for fixed income, conditions support compelling returns. Still, a weakening labor market and tight credit spreads present potential challenges. We see 2026 shaping up to be a year in which investors can find value by locking in yield, managing duration and focusing on diversification — while keeping a close eye on credit quality.

The most glaring uncertainty heading into 2026 is the course of Fed monetary policy. For the Fed, there will be the challenge of navigating a labor market that could either rebound or deteriorate further. In addition, there remain questions for the Fed surrounding inflation and growth, such as any direct and secondary effects of tariffs on inflation, the acceleration in private-sector capital spending, the easing of financial conditions and the positive fiscal impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

The tension between labor market softness and ongoing economic growth will define the bond market’s path in 2026. In this uncertain environment, the key investment opportunities reside among securities offering attractive yield per unit of duration. Asset-based finance stands out as an area of value. Backed by healthy household balance sheets and secured with collateral, consumer-oriented bonds offer diversification away from traditional corporate credit.

Another key opportunity for active fixed-income investors in 2026 will be to capitalize on growing credit dispersion. After several years of monitoring, we are now seeing expanding differences in credit performance across sectors and issuers. Our credit-research intensity, paired with our highly active and dynamic style, will allow us to pursue these growing active-return opportunities.

data.title: BlackRock Strategic Global Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 0.58% (Institutional shares) and 0.70% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • Developed market currencies and emerging market spreads and currencies contributed to performance, while U.S. high yield credit and U.S. rates positions detracted.
  • We tactically increased duration (interest rate sensitivity) to an overweight position, notably by raising exposure to emerging market rates. Amid economic resilience, the fund maintained its overweight positions in the U.S. dollar and emerging market currencies. It continued to hold an up-in-quality bias across global credit sectors.

Contributors

The fund's currency positioning contributed to performance. Within developed market currencies, an underweight allocation to the Japanese yen was beneficial as it depreciated amid the prospect of slower interest rate hikes from the Bank of Japan. The overweight position in the U.S. dollar was additive given economic resilience. The overweight exposure to emerging market currencies contributed as, broadly, these continued to appreciate amid supportive technical conditions and fundamentals. Similarly, emerging market spreads, particularly credit, were helpful due to tighter spreads and attractive all-in yields.

Detractors

An out-of-benchmark allocation to U.S. credit detracted during the quarter. Additionally, the U.S. rates position weighed on performance, driven by the underweight exposure to the front end of the yield curve. The allocation performed weakly in October as the yield curve rallied following softer Consumer Price Index data and as the Federal Reserve continued to cut interest rates.

Further insight

During the quarter, we increased top-line duration to an overweight position (6.28 years versus the benchmark's 6.18 years), mainly by raising the overweight exposure to emerging market rates. In emerging market rates, we preferred to own countries with resilient growth and easing monetary policy. In U.S. rates, we built in a steepening bias, holding an overweight position in the front and the "belly" of the yield curve. We maintained overweight allocations to the U.S. dollar and emerging market currencies, and held underweight allocations to the euro and yen. In spreads, we tactically rotated across sectors as we continued to seek attractive, global sources of income while remaining cautious about lower-quality credit.

data.title: Invesco Rochester AMT-Free New York Municipal Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Fund performance

Invesco Rochester® AMT-Free New York Municipal Fund Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) underperformed its style-specific index, the S&P Municipal Bond New York 5+ Year Investment Grade Index.

2 Seeking attractive opportunities through collaborative management

Invesco Municipal Bond team uses a collaborative management approach. Relying on our size and experience, we seek to identify the best opportunities to achieve potentially better outcomes for shareholders.

3 Analysis focused on creditworthiness

Our team uses a bottom-up fundamental credit process focused on creditworthiness of individual issuers with an overlay of macroeconomic factors to capitalize on market inefficiencies. Our process has been time tested over full market cycles.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • In the fourth quarter, investment grade, high yield and taxable municipals delivered positive returns of 1.42%, 1.11% and 1.05%, with annual returns of 4.25%, 2.46% and 7.89%, respectively. 1 Despite a lengthy federal government shutdown, long-duration municipal bonds performed well during the quarter, bolstering market strength. 1
  • New municipal issuance reached $143 billion for the quarter and a record $584 billion for the year, surpassing last year's record $509 billion. Shifting interest rate policies, higher costs and political uncertainty likely encouraged more issuers to come to market. 1
  • Net flows for municipal mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) were strong, totaling approximately $17.5 billion for the quarter and $52.4 billion for the year. 2
  • The US Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the federal funds rate twice, by 0.25% in October and 0.25% in December. The Fed reiterated its commitment to balancing maximum employment and a 2% inflation target. 3
  • We believe state and local municipal budgets remain healthy. While credit rating upgrades have moderated, upgrades outpaced downgrades in 2025, demonstrating to us strong fundamentals. 4
  • Our experienced credit research team continues to seek out market dislocations, uncovering opportunities to add value for shareholders.
  • Looking ahead, we see attractive opportunities in municipals. With prospects for more Fed interest rate cuts, steady issuance and ongoing demand for tax-exempt income, we believe high absolute yields and solid fundamentals make municipals a compelling choice.

Portfolio positioning

Yield Curve and Duration Positioning: Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook on duration and see attractive opportunities to increase duration following the steepening of the municipal yield curve in 2025. We plan to selectively add longer duration securities to the fund when appropriate, having found particular value in the long end of the yield curve, specifically maturities of 15 to 20 years.

Sector Allocations: We maintain an overweight position in revenue bonds relative to state and local general obligation bonds, though we note that pension risks historically associated with general obligation bonds have generally been mitigated over the past several years. Within the revenue bond segment, the fund's largest exposures are in the dedicated tax, tobacco settlement and higher education sectors.

Credit Conditions: We maintain a positive view on fundamentals in the municipal market, as credit rating upgrades have continued to outpace downgrades. While various sectors have come under pressure due to reduced federal support, inflation-driven cost pressures and negative political headlines, we continue to rely on our extensive bottom-up research capabilities to identify strong credits with attractive yields.

Performance highlights

The fourth quarter began on a strong note despite record levels of new issuance and the longest federal government shutdown in history. Economic signals were mixed, with solid Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth alongside persistent inflation and a weakening employment outlook. The Fed cut the federal funds target rate by 0.25% in both October and December, for a total of three rate cuts in 2025. Municipals rallied in October and delivered steady, positive returns through the end of the quarter. Yields on the longer end of the municipal yield curve declined, with 10- and 30-year bond yields falling by 0.19% and 0.11%, respectively. 5 Overall, higher quality municipal credits generally outperformed during the quarter.

Contributors to performance

Contributors to relative return this quarter included the following:

An overweight allocation and security selection in zero-coupon bonds added to relative performance. The fund's overweights in non-rated bonds and bonds rated AA also contributed to relative performance.

Detractors from performance

Detractors from relative return this quarter included the following:

An overweight in bonds with durations between 10 and 15 years detracted from relative performance, as did security selection among bonds in this duration segment. The fund's security selection in AA-rated bonds and in the water and sewer sector also detracted from relative performance.

data.title: XMVM: Impressive Value Characteristics But Unappealing Quality And Performance
data.description:
I believe that at this juncture, there are investors who might be considering the Invesco S&P MidCap Value with Momentum ETF (XMVM), a passively managed exchange-traded fund offering exposure to the combination of seemingly antithetical factors, for a number of reasons.

  • For instance, if they are betting on the capital rotation to the size factor and cyclicals that we have seen earlier this year before the Iran war to resume.
  • Or if they want to downsize their exposure to the large-cap echelon and the growth factor and rotate to the raciest value stocks, a seemingly safer place to be amid the conflict and its ripple effects for inflation and growth premia.

And while these arguments certainly make sense on the surface, my intention today is to prove that XMVM does not offer either a sizable upside potential or at least a prospect of a lower theoretical maximum drawdown compared to that of the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV).

More specifically, I see two main problems with this vehicle.

  • Quality characteristics of its portfolio are quite soft even by the mid-cap echelon standards. And quality is the factor I favor in highly uncertain environments with economic shocks becoming increasingly possible. It cannot immunize portfolios from losses (especially in a scenario the market is currently in) but can at least make them less acute.
  • There is a lot to dislike about XMVM's performance since the strategy change in June 2019, especially regarding the maximum drawdowns, which I pay close attention to in the current market conditions.

In this regard, I am skeptical that it will be capable of outmaneuvering either the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF or the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) in the market environment we see today. And even though I am pleasantly surprised by XMVM's GARP tilt, I initiate coverage with a Hold rating.

XMVM Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the Invesco website, XMVM "...is based on the S&P MidCap 400 High Momentum Value Index." However, as explained in the fact sheet, this index was adopted only in June 2019. And before that, the fund changed its underlying index three times since its inception in 2005:

  • Before June 16, 2011, it tracked the Dynamic Mid Cap Value Intellidex Index.
  • From June 16, 2011 to May 22, 2015, its underlying index was the RAFI Fundamental Mid Value Index.
  • And from May 22, 2015 to June 21, 2019, the ETF tracked the Russell Midcap Pure Value Index.

So, given the indices have materially different methodologies, it is obvious we have to ignore performance data prior to the June 2019 strategy change.

How is the current index calibrated? It is explained that this benchmark

... is composed of 80 securities in the S&P MidCap 400 Index having both the highest "value scores" and "momentum scores". The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted semi-annually on the third Friday of June and December.

As detailed in the summary prospectus, the value score is based on

...(i) book value-to-price ratio, calculated using the company’s latest book value per share divided by its price; (ii) earnings-to-price ratio, calculated using the company’s trailing 12-month earnings per share divided by its price; and (iii) sales-to-price ratio, calculated using the company’s trailing 12-month sales per share divided by its price.

At the same time, momentum is measured using

...the percentage change in the stock’s price over the last 12 months, excluding the most recent month, and applying an adjustment based on the security’s volatility over that period.

Owing to the demanding selection criteria and relatively frequent reconstitution, the fund's turnover is fairly high at 73%.

What is the product of this strategy? I would argue that it mainly resulted in excellent value characteristics of the portfolio. However, quality suffered a lot. So, overall, I am not content with exposures I see.

So, as of March 23, there were 78 common stocks and REITs in the XMVM portfolio, with all of them, unsurprisingly, being present in the IJH portfolio (given it tracks the S&P 400) as well (and XMVM is overweight in all of them). However, in the latter, they have just 18% weight, as per my calculations.

Company Ticker Weight XMVM, as of March 23 Weight in IJH, as of March 20 Sector Market Cap, $ Billion, as of March 24
Lear Corporation (LEA) 2.64% 0.18% Consumer Discretionary 5.757
PBF Energy Inc. (PBF) 2.41% 0.13% Energy 5.809
Brighthouse Financial, Inc. (BHF) 2.24% 0.11% Financials 3.394
HF Sinclair Corporation (DINO) 2.21% 0.30% Energy 10.856
TD SYNNEX Corporation (SNX) 2.18% 0.37% Information Technology 12.317
Valaris Limited (VAL) 2.13% 0.19% Energy 6.405
Albertsons Companies, Inc. (ACI) 2.07% 0.20% Consumer Staples 8.886
RenaissanceRe Holdings Ltd. (RNR) 2.03% 0.40% Financials 12.546
Graham Holdings Company (GHC) 1.80% 0.11% Consumer Discretionary 4.523
Macy's, Inc. (M) 1.76% 0.15% Consumer Discretionary 4.700

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

Looking at the key ten companies, we see just two financial names, BHF and RNR, which might make an impression that this portfolio is rather light in the sector. But thinking so is a mistake. The reality is that around 43.7% of its net assets are allocated to the sector (which is more than the second-, third-, fourth- and fifth-largest sectors combined).

And this tells a lot about what factor story to expect—given how compressed are P/E ratios in the sector, it is no surprise that XMVM's adjusted weighted average earnings yield is around 1.9x larger than IJH's, as per my calculations.

However, a remark worth making here is that XMVM is a high-turnover vehicle, and it is reasonable to expect sector exposures to drift in the future. Still, I think it is unlikely that financials will be dethroned as the sector has inherently strong value characteristics compared to the market (which is nicely illustrated by its 12.3x median TTM P/E ratio as of writing this article). And the sector was the ETF's top allocation even in 2019, although with a smaller share of the net assets: data from ETFDb.com saved by the Wayback Machine shows that in July 2019, financials were the primary allocation with 21.59% weight followed by industrials (20.7%).

So, on the one hand, the financial sector as the core allocation could be beneficial; for instance, amid high interest rates, the S&P MidCap 400 Financials index declined by only 3.04% in 2022 vs. 18.16% decline of IVV. On the other hand, I believe that in current circumstances it could be more of a drag, as the drawdown I expect in the worst-case scenario would be driven by investors pricing in a recession as well, and here, SMID financials are at risk of significant underperformance.

After this brief digression, let us return to the ratios. We see that other metrics like Price/Sales and EV/EBITDA are well lower than those of IJH while the allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher is more than 2x larger.

What is my view on the value factor's potential in this market? I am mildly skeptical. Some selectiveness in this space might reward investors as inexpensiveness could secure a more comfortable drawdown compared to the market. But for XMVM, I believe this is not in the cards, as historical data does not support that hypothesis (I will be addressing this in greater depth shortly).

Next, while the XMVM portfolio has a way less compelling growth story (for instance, its WA forward revenue growth rate is less than 6%), XMVM's impressive value characteristics secured its GARP tilt, as the metrics below illustrate.

Metric XMVM IJH
PEG Ratio 0.8894 1.4464
Price/Sales-to-Growth Ratio 0.3324 0.3853
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 0.8126 1.4791

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

At the same time, XMVM is significantly behind the S&P 400 ETF on quality, a vulnerability I believe is critical in the current highly volatile environment. More specifically, IJH's allocation to stocks with at least a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher is already rather low at only 64.17%. But XMVM's allocation is almost 1.7x smaller. Besides, XMVM's capital efficiency indicators are considerably weaker than those of IJH.

Finally, even though the ETF is focused on the raciest value stocks (which, in theory, should have been supportive of high beta), it has a tilt towards the low volatility factor as illustrated by the weighted average beta coefficients. This could slightly support its performance in a highly uncertain environment, but I do not believe this will be able to offset the impact of low quality.

XMVM Performance: Risk Metrics Are Concerning

Performance-wise, my main concern is not XMVM's annualized or cumulative returns (though there is something to dislike here as well). The issues are chiefly related to its risk metrics (i.e., maximum drawdowns and the downside capture ratio).

So, looking at its performance since June 2019, we see quite an appealing picture, as XMVM has outmaneuvered both IJH and the iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value ETF (IJJ), even though keeping pace with tech- and growth-heavy IVV was too complicated for it to achieve.

But let us look at the annualized data for the July 2019–February 2026 period.

Here, we see an impressive CAGR that is well higher than those of IJH and IJJ, as well as strong risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios). Despite all that, XMVM had a maximum drawdown of -35.24%, the deepest in the group (and it captured over 116% of IVV's downside). And I would like to give this issue a bit more attention.

More specifically, I see roughly three types of XMVM's maximum drawdowns:

  • the pandemic type (March 2020, -35.24%)—driven by an almost unrepeatable event threatening to derail global economic growth.
  • the 2022 bear market type (September 2022, -19.77%)—chiefly driven by high interest rates (and inflation); here, XMVM had a weaker drawdown than IJJ, yet IVV and IJH did worse.
  • the tariff drama type (April 2025, -15.62%)—in part similar to the pandemic type yet less acute, though also assuming some economic pain driven by inflation and stressed supply chains. In this scenario, XMVM had the weakest maximum drawdown compared to all selected ETFs.

As the Iran war is not over and the Strait of Hormuz question is not resolved, what type of maximum drawdown should we expect from XMVM in a worst-case scenario with oil prices remaining stubbornly high? I surmise it might be something similar to the first and second types, which means XMVM will possibly underperform the market proxied with IVV as well as IJH and IJJ.

XMVM AUM, Liquidity, Expenses

Speaking of liquidity, XMVM has assets under management of $415.78 million, which is, of course, decent but not impressive given the fund has more than 21 year in the books. Also, the ETF has a rather high net expense ratio of 39 bps. For context, the iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value ETF's ER is more than 2x lower at 18 bps.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, I believe XMVM offers a deeply undervalued portfolio and some surprising GARP characteristics, yet its performance (especially its maximum drawdowns) as well as quality characteristics are not compelling enough to merit a Buy rating in the current environment, so I initiate coverage with the Hold rating.

data.title: Ermenegildo Zegna: Limited Upside Amid Macro Uncertainty
data.description:
On 20 March 2026, Ermenegildo Zegna N.V. (ZGN) released its 2025 numbers. As usual, we are back to comment on the quarterly results and 2026 expectations.

In our last assessment, we reported how Zegna’s share price approached our valuation range, leaving limited upside. This was a positive call, even if we were lucky to report a neutral rate before the escalation in the Middle East (Fig. 1 with -5.37% since early Jan 2026). On a fundamental basis, our neutral view was driven by ongoing restructuring at Thom Browne, continued weakness in the wholesale channel, the absence of supportive trends in Greater China, and management’s reference to a “new normal” rather than a near-term rebound. This proved a more cautious outlook in our estimates.

Fig 1

Zegna Results and Our Neutral View

Starting with the 2025 numbers, the company reported solid results (even relative to peers). Thanks to our cross-coverage analysis in Sanlorenzo, 2025 luxury revenues trended down by an average of 3.7%, while Zegna reported top-line sales of €1.91 billion (Fig. 2), down 1.5% year-over-year but up 1.1% on an organic basis. Divisionally, the company reported higher sales generation in the core brand (+4.7%) and Tom Ford (+3.1%). Thom Browne's revenues and the textile segments were down 12.2% organically and 3.1%, respectively. Zegna reported a higher gross profit margin, thanks to its core brand having a higher MIX and a higher direct-to-consumer channel, rising to 82% at a 67.5% margin. SG&A increased due to 1) higher personnel expenses and IT costs, 2) network expansion, and 3) a negative $10 million one-off on expected losses in the trade receivables. For this reason, the 2025 adjusted EBIT declined to €162.9 million from €184 million achieved in 2024. Thanks to solid FCF generation and sales of Temasek treasury shares, the company achieved a positive net financial position of €52 million. Going down to the P&L, Zegna recorded a positive financial income. With an FX gain of €9m, Zegna ended the year with a net profit of €109 million compared to 2024 results of €90.8 million.

Fig 2

Why are we Neutral?

Starting with the Middle East escalation, the company reported that the area "is a relevant region for our group and for the Zegna brand in particular." According to the annual report, Zegna includes the Middle East in its EMEA reporting. As we write the analysis, we are witnessing a de-escalation, but we believe it is too soon to be positive. Given disruptions to local clients and global tourists, luxury companies might be affected. Zegna sells entirely by retail, and we expect a negative margin MIX contribution. Even if the sales impact ultimately depends on the severity and duration of the conflict, there will be a sales disruption in the area.

During the Q&A analyst call, management noted that 2026 started well and DTC trends were positive. Chinese New Year was said to have come slightly ahead of expectations, while the Americas region (the US and LatAm) was reported to have good growth. While expectations are muted in the EU, in the Middle East, the company confirmed that stores are open (following a brief closure), but consumption is softer. Still related to the sales MIX, sales in the Greater China Region further decelerated by 13.8% in Q4 (Fig. 3). This confirmed our thesis on a Chinese “new normal.”

Fig 3

In terms of profitability, the company appears pleased with the yearly development. We report a 82% DTC retail MIX, and this remains a key driver of gross margin evolution. Still, we need to see Thom Browne EBIT return to positive territory (Fig. 4) following a negative H2 2025, and Tom Ford Fashion EBIT double-digit. Again, we believe the company is more focused on protecting its margins than on expanding its top line.

Fig 4

Zegna has not provided 2026 guidance, so we believe the top management team is in a wait-and-see mode. Regarding the top-line FX, the company confirms that the latest moves are favourable but may remain a headwind for the year.

Valuation

We were right to move our 2025 top-line sales close to €1.9 billion versus a previous estimate of €2 billion. At this stage, we believe the Middle East impact could result in a 2/3% sales decline, considering a 3-month conflict and software consumption in the future. At the EBIT margin, this effect could prove more pronounced. This is based on the fact that Zegna's core brand is only retail. That said, there is no margin for MIX visibility in the area. Last time, we incorporated a 4% sales increase, but even accounting for the positive sales trend before the escalation in the Middle East, we are now more cautious. We will see positive results if Zegna's sales are flattish compared to 2025 results.

On the other hand, despite a negative EBIT evolution from the Middle East, the company has 1) upside from its gross margin given the intention to increase DTC sales (from 82% to 85%), and 2) lower D&A given less CAPEX investment in 2025 vs historical average (2025 CAPEX of €102.8 million vs 2024 CAPEX of €125.5 million).

Going down to the P&L, we also include a positive financial income due to lower interest payments from -€10m to +€2.5m. All in all, our EBIT margin is set at 9%, with a 2026 results expectation of €172.5 million. With a corporate tax rate of 25%, our net profit estimates increased from €125.25 million to €129.4 million. This implied an EPS of €0.48, based on the current outstanding shares.

Applying no change to our FWD P/E of 20x, Zegna's target price reached €9.6 and $11.4 in ADR. Considering the current valuation of $10.2 per share, there is limited upside. We prefer to remain neutral.

Risks

We have long covered Zegna, so our main downside risks are lower consumer sentiment, given ongoing worldwide conflicts (and potential escalations), and FX changes. The company is impacted by negative results from two divisions, Tom Ford and Thom Browne, due to restructuring activities. These, again, might limit the company's margin and valuation. Last time, we reported Zegna's sneaker sales as % of the total turnover. Moreover, Zegna continues to lose momentum in China. Sector-wide valuation and a deterioration in luxury sentiment might penalize Zegna's share price evolution.

Conclusion

At the Lab, we don't believe this is the right time to invest in a luxury company like Zegna. The company is well-run, but market sentiment and headwinds are present. Given limited visibility in 2026, we prefer to wait for a better entry point.

data.title: Fortinet's Edge In A Weak Cybersecurity Market
data.description:
Since I last issued a Buy rating for Fortinet (FTNT) in September last year, there has been a wider sell-off in the SaaS and cybersecurity space with some stocks like Zscaler correcting by up to 50%. The corrections have been severe, and most of these stocks are not in a mood to revert anytime soon - unless long-duration growth assumptions get more certainties around how the evolved landscape will be in a native AI world.

Fortinet has been flat since the time of my September thesis, resilient through this sell-off. This thesis review looks at Fortinet in this evolved regime where pricing pressures and competitive moat are being viewed differently to see whether Fortinet's resilience is accidental and whether the Buy rating is still valid, or whether a sell-off is simply delayed. My analysis shows that most of the factors that I was bullish on in September have only strengthened since then, and Fortinet's resilience is actually structural. In fact, one of the key drags in September was firewall cycle concerns - growth drivers now seem to have shifted toward platform-led expansion across SASE, AI and SecOps, further strengthening the Buy thesis.

Why Fortinet Has Held Up

The chart below shows how the cybersecurity pack has sold off across the board, while Fortinet is just down ~5% over the past 6 months. The reasons for this relative outperformance start from valuations. For high-growth and SaaS-heavy players like CrowdStrike, Palo Alto and Zscaler, valuations were elevated (and still remain premium even after corrections). As growth expectations normalized and AI-related disruption risks emerged, these higher multiple names saw sharper multiple compression. In contrast, names like Fortinet, Okta and Check Point were already trading at more reasonable valuations, limiting the extent of further de-rating.

The AI disruption we are talking about today is centered around commoditization and pricing power. Pricing power was already a topic in 2025 as platform consolidation threats were visible. Larger players like Microsoft are already bundling security with broader offerings, making the moat less compelling for standalone security vendors - especially those in identity, endpoint and cloud security. AI-led fears have meant that the pricing power question becomes even more relevant. AI can accelerate innovation cycles and even automate parts of the security functions, reducing the need for multiple point solutions over time, or at least reducing their pricing power.

Fortinet's relative resilience is both structural and because the stock had already taken a hit ahead of the wider sell-off driven by a completely different set of concerns (firewall cycle refresh concerns and some margin compression fears). The September thesis had talked about how the markets were concerned about the fact that a large part of the upgrade cycle had already been digested, pulling forward demand and increasing risks of a near-term slowdown. At the same time, increased R&D and sales investments created concerns around operating margin pressure.

We will address Fortinet's intrinsic thesis in the next section and allay those concerns that were at play in September (when it was still a Buy despite the concerns). But one of the factors that helped Fortinet's resilient performance in the recent sell-off was that it was already trading at lower valuations, avoiding the haircut that the rest of the pack received.

There are more important structural factors that explain this resilience too, in my opinion. One, the core business is network security and hardware-led infrastructure, not purely SaaS (demand is tied to installed base and infrastructure refresh cycles, which are less assumption-heavy in today's cautious regime than purely forward ARR expectations). Two, it is less exposed to AI disruption risks that directly impact endpoint, identity and cloud-native vendors. That is, the positioning is slightly different and not necessarily stronger - but enough to stay resilient and insulated from the wider cybersecurity sell-off.

Intrinsic Thesis Has Strengthened

With the relative bigger picture established, let us move to the pillars of the September Buy thesis to check whether they remain valid supportive factors for a Buy.

Structural cybersecurity tailwinds are not only intact, but they have actually been strengthened. According to Fortinet's Q4 2025 earnings call:

This opportunity is supported by strong secular tailwinds such as vendor consolidation, the convergence of security and networking, ongoing technology upgrades and the expansion of enterprise attack surfaces across cloud, OT and AI.

Fortinet is still seeing broad-based demand across segments and geographies. AI and OT (Operational Technology) are in fact new demand vectors for the company.

Topline growth numbers remain intact too. Revenue was up by ~15% (billings up 18% YoY) and is guided to grow by 12-13% overall in FY2026. This confirms my low teens steady growth outlook. Not a re-acceleration story, but still supportive.

The platform positioning (network + SASE + SecOps) as a growth engine remains valid too. Customers are increasingly consolidating multiple vendors onto Fortinet's platform. This is enabled by its single OS architecture integrating firewall, SD-WAN and SASE, at a third of the total cost of ownership compared to peers.

Importantly, the refresh cycle fears have diluted, and the growth thesis is now tied to adjacencies and platform expansion. Unified SASE continues to scale rapidly with ~40% billings growth, Security Operations grew ~22% for the full year (ARR up ~21%), and newer areas like OT security are also contributing meaningfully with ~25% growth. With more AI use at enterprises, demand is further supported as companies need more security around internal segmentation or data leakage risks. All of this means there is a lot of scope of expanding use cases within the installed base, and refresh cycles are not the single most important factor that looms over Fortinet.

The business model is also showing signs of strength in recurring revenue as ARR continues to scale. Services now account for two thirds of the revenue. Despite continued investments across ASIC, AI and infrastructure, profitability remains structurally strong, with operating margins in the ~35–37% range. Free cash flow margins stay above 30%. That is a healthy zone at scale from a Rule of 40 perspective that combines margins and growth together (Rule of 45 plus at the moment).

Maintain Buy

Since Fortinet's intrinsic thesis has not weakened since September (in fact, it strengthened in a lot of areas like new demand vectors from OT and AI), the question revolves around whether its resilience now is actually a setup for delayed corrections or a sign of structural strength. Throughout this thesis I do not find any evidence for the former outcome. Pricing power also appears intact, with selective increases in the 5-20% range supported by a clear cost advantage.

In fact, what has additionally happened since September is that the central drag to the thesis (refresh cycle fears) has diluted. While earlier concerns around the firewall cycle were directionally valid, they are becoming less central to the story today. I would therefore recommend fresh buys at this point, expecting further gains as platform-driven expansion, cross-selling across SASE and SecOps, and new demand vectors in AI and OT start to reflect in sustained billing growth and stronger recurring revenue. That could see some rerating kick in as Fortinet starts being viewed less as a cyclical hardware story and more as a platform-led compounder.

data.title: Constellation Energy: Nuclear And Electric Demand Strength Is Going Nowhere
data.description:
Constellation Energy (CEG) is one of the largest power producers in the United States and the nation’s leading supplier of carbon-free electricity. They have a dominant position in nuclear power, which is increasingly emerging as one of, if not the most trustworthy, clean energy sources across markets, and specifically in the rapid growth mode toward AI data centers.

That’s where the discussion lies this week, as CEG has been presented as one of the suppliers for power in collaboration with NVIDIA (NVDA) and Emerald AI in the coming years of their data center plans.

The stock traded higher on Monday morning as the news surfaced and pulled back by trading day’s close. I gave CEG a buy about a year ago, and the stock has returned 36% since. I switched my rating to a hold this past fall, as I felt the stock needed a slight correction on raised valuation and dragging margins.

I think the time has returned again as a buy scenario for CEG. Some fundamentals are still lagging a bit, and that would be my biggest concern for the moment. But the stock has corrected over 25% from its high this past fall and is surrounded by a plethora of promising and exciting news, including the recent possible NVDA partnership, as well as some secured long-term contracts with other huge data center players. This is the future, and CEG has positioned themselves in the middle of a period seeking long-term and clean electric demand.

NVDA & Emerald AI Outlook

The big news this week coming from CERAWeek in Houston is the development of a partnership between NVDA, Emerald AI, and several big U.S. energy companies like CEG. The goal is to develop next-generation AI factories that can operate flexibly on the power grid by adjusting usage in response to grid conditions as needed.

This is suggested to be done through NVDA’s Vera Rubin DSX architecture with Emerald AI’s conductor platform. Energy providers like CEG have massive long-term revenue potential here to provide onsite power for years ahead to one of the biggest companies in the world and a leader in the broader tech/AI space.

The biggest difference with this approach versus standard data center energy is in this route; the energy assets allow for facilities to grab power quicker and more efficiently through co-located generation and storage. The flexibility is huge for data centers that need various levels of power on and off through the course of a day, week, or month.

Why CEG Stands Out

Other energy-providing peers like AES, Vistra (VST), and NextEra Energy (NEE) are also said to be in on the new relationship with NVDA.

This is not to say I don’t like those stocks on this news, but for me, I see CEG as a particularly good opportunity. Each of these firms operates slightly differently on the clean energy scale. CEG is the frontrunner among the group in the nuclear space, which I have advocated as my favorite long-term clean energy solution. Lifecycle emissions are extremely low, and energy output per fuel amount is greater than many other renewables. The biggest piece to my advocacy of nuclear is that it can reliably run 24/7, unlike the other clean sources. Consistency and reliability will be vital for the amount of data running through these centers. CEG has 21 nuclear reactors across several states and produces over 180,000 GWh of nuclear generation a year. These peers have strengths in other energy avenues, but when it comes to nuclear, no one is close. I predict that NVDA, Emerald AI, and other AI data center seekers are going to see that nuclear is the clear best option for long-haul energy success.

Contracts in Place

In recent years, CEG has done a fantastic job picking up big revenue contracts on long-term deals. A restart of the Crane Clean Energy Center for Microsoft (MSFT) for 835 MW of nuclear energy for mainly data centers is in place. One of the largest nuclear deals ever made, for 1.1 GW nuclear power with Meta, is slated to begin in 2027. They have a reliable contract in place with the U.S. government on a 10-year, $840 million contract that is already supplying the firm revenue. Lastly, they have made a recent deal with CyrusOne for 380 MW of power for a new data center in Texas.

Outside of the government contract, revenues related to these other deals are expected to be realized by 2027 and for decades after. Larger companies seeking data center power seem to be looking toward nuclear more and more. CEG is gaining trust in the market as they pick up more and more contracts from big-name players. These contracts also lead to long-term and stable revenue, usually for decades.

Valuation and Margins

The risk for me with CEG is the current value and direction that margins have been running. With a PE of 39x, valuation is still a little inflated for CEG. Compared to the couple-year history and the sector median, I think a fairer value is in the low 30s for PE ratio. Earnings have gained over recent years but have been a little subdued over the last few quarters. I expect earnings to ramp up as these contracts in place come to life. It may still be another year or so, but with the dip the stock has seen, I still think it’s a good time to buy.

Margins have also been dragging in recent quarters for CEG. One of the bigger concerns with nuclear is the higher upfront and operating costs for these plants. It is often much more expensive than other clean power sources to operate, and expenses have eaten at their operating margin, which has dropped from 21% to 15.75% in the last year. Net income margin is 9%, just below the industry average. I am not as concerned about this for now, but if margin issues remain once several of these data center deals begin, then I will become a little leery if nuclear is too costly.

Conclusion

I think CEG is in a really strong place for great return over the next decade, as nuclear power grows on heavy electric demand, specifically from massive data centers popping up all over. It’s a competitive space for electric companies, but I see CEG as a frontrunner, having distinguished themselves with their nuclear dominance. Contracts that are already in place have them set for a revenue ramp-up beginning next year. The recent news that has broken on a possible partnership with NVDA and Emerald AI is also massive for CEG. This is still in very early stages but is another huge steppingstone in gaining trust from big-time companies for huge power demand. CEG is evolving from an old, boring utility into a critical supplier for the AI economy. Buy this stock on the recent correction and long-term industry outlook and demand.
